ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 'carbonated beverage' is the closest parent class. No exact FoodOn term exists for generic canned soft drink as a category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0KN 'Soft drinks' is the closest parent category for generic canned soft drinks. No more specific FoodEx2 code for canned carbonated soft drink as a generic category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml aluminium can, the typical single-serve portion sold at cafes and convenience stores in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated beverage, cola-type — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 14400)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Generic canned soft drink nutrient profile adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for carbonated cola-type soft drink (approximately 39 kcal/100 ml, 10 g sugar/100 ml, negligible protein and fat). Values represent a typical cola-type canned soft drink. Actual values vary by brand and flavour. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation for standard cola-type carbonated soft drinks.

gi_source.title: Coca-Cola, carbonated soft drink — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cola-type carbonated soft drink (Coca-Cola GI ~53, glucose reference). This is used as the closest analogous estimate for a generic canned soft drink. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 33.0) / 100 = 17.5, where carbohydrate per 330 ml serving = 10.0 g/100 ml × 3.3 = 33.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~4 mg/100 ml; ~13 mg per 330 ml can)
hypertension.guidance: Canned soft drinks are very low in sodium and do not directly contribute to elevated blood pressure through sodium load; however, high sugar intake from regular consumption may contribute to weight gain and metabolic risk factors associated with hypertension, so moderation is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (~33 g per 330 ml can) and moderate glycemic index (~53)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid regular canned soft drinks due to their high free sugar content and moderate glycemic index, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; sugar-free or diet variants should be considered as alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content linked to dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and elevated triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened canned soft drinks is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and insulin resistance; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict intake in line with WHO guidelines on free sugar consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A canned soft drink (罐装软饮料) is a commercially manufactured, carbonated, sugar-sweetened beverage sold in a sealed aluminium can, typically in 330 ml single-serve portions. In Singapore, canned soft drinks are widely available at cafes, hawker centres, convenience stores, and food delivery platforms, commonly priced around SGD 2.00. The beverage is characterised by its effervescent texture, sweet taste, and variety of flavours including cola, lemon-lime, orange, and root beer. Nutritionally, a standard cola-type canned soft drink provides approximately 39 kcal per 100 ml, almost entirely from free sugars (~10 g/100 ml), with negligible protein and fat. A single 330 ml can delivers approximately 33 g of free sugar, exceeding the WHO recommended daily free sugar limit of 25 g for adults. The glycemic index of cola-type soft drinks is approximately 53 (low-moderate range), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~17.5) due to the large sugar volume per can. Clinically, regular consumption is associated with elevated postprandial blood glucose, insulin resistance, weight gain, dyslipidaemia, and increased cardiovascular risk. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease are advised to limit or avoid regular sugar-sweetened canned soft drinks. Diet or zero-sugar variants are available as lower-glycaemic alternatives. The product is ultra-processed, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Common ingredients include carbonated water, high-fructose corn syrup or sucrose, caramel colour, phosphoric acid, natural flavours, and caffeine.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of soft drink?
Is canned soft drink bad for diabetes?
Can I drink soft drinks if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a generic cola-type carbonated soft drink, which is the closest approved source match. Actual values vary by brand and flavour. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cola-type soft drinks. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for generic canned soft drinks as a category.
notes: This record represents a generic canned soft drink category entry, not a specific branded product. Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for carbonated cola-type soft drink and should be treated as representative estimates. protein_g is confirmed zero — contains no protein by product formulation for standard carbonated soft drinks. fat_g is confirmed zero — contains no fat by product formulation for standard carbonated soft drinks. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, dietary_fibre_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this food category. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source for this food. glycemic_index is set to 53 based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cola-type carbonated soft drink; gi_category is low (GI 53 ≤ 55). The input cuisine tag 'Italian' reflects the restaurant context (The Light Cafe) and is not the cuisine classification of the beverage itself; this record is classified as Global/Generic. field survey data source URL retained as provenance of the original seed record only.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from menu-style 'Soft/Can Drinks 罐装饮料' to canonical English 'Canned Soft Drink'.
Added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Singapore context.
Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g=0 and fat_g=0 (confirmed zero by product formulation).
Set glycemic_index=53 (numeric >0) and gi_category='low' (GI 53 ≤ 55) per GI threshold rules.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit source-not-reported justifications.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency — all language entries present verbatim in flat aliases.
Corrected cuisine from 'Italian' (restaurant context) to 'Global / Generic' for the beverage category.
Used USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved secondary source; field survey URL retained as seed provenance only.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: exact, closest_parent, or none.
